coredata.dc:title: Lemon-derived carbon dots as antioxidant and light emitter in fluorescent films applied to nanothermometry
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: da Silva, Livia E.
Calado, Orlando Lucas de L.
de Oliveira Silva, Steffano Felix
da Silva, Kleyton Ritomar Monteiro
Henrique Almeida, James
de Oliveira Silva, Messias
Viana, Rodrigo da Silva
de Souza Ferro, Jamylle Nunes
de Almeida Xavier, Jadriane
Barbosa, Cintya. D.A.E.S.
coredata.dc:description: The design of luminescent nanomaterials for the development of nanothermometers with high sensitivity and free of potentially toxic metals has developed in several fields, such as optoelectronics, sensors, and bioimaging. In addition, luminescent nanothermometers have advantages related to non-invasive measurement, with their wide detection range and high spatial resolution at the nano/microscale. Our study is the first, to our knowledge, to demonstrate a detailed study of a fluorescent film (Film-L) thermal sensor based on carbon dots derived from lemon bagasse extract (CD-L). The CD-L properties were explored as an antioxidant agent; their cytotoxicity was evaluated by using a human non-tumoral skin fibroblast (HFF-1) cell line from an MTT assay. The CD-L were characterized by HRTEM, DLS, FTIR, UV–VIS, and fluorescence spectroscopy. These confirmed their particle size distribution below 10 nm, graphitic structure in the core and surface organic groups, and strong blue emission. The CD-L showed cytocompatibility behavior and scavenging potential reactive species of biological importance: O2 •− and HOCl, with IC50 of 276.8 ± 4.0 and 21.6 ± 0.7, respectively. The Film-L emission intensities (I425 nm) are temperature-dependent in the 298 to 333 K range. The Film-L luminescent thermometer shows a maximum relative thermal sensitivity of 2.69 % K−1 at 333 K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon dots
Antioxidant
Nanothermometer
Film

Temperature-Dependent Photoluminescence properties of the Film-L

Oliveira dos Santos and Laboratório Multiusuário de Microscopia de Alta Resolução (LabMic) of University of Goiás, Brazil, for HRTEM measurements. The English text of this paper has been revised by Sidney Pratt, Canadian, MAT (The Johns Hopkins University), RSAdip - TESL (Cambridge University).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A-B) HRTEM images, C) HRTEM image and interplanar distance for (100) plane, and D) Particle size distribution for CD-L.
(A) Aqueous dispersion after and before ultraviolet light exposure (B) Photoluminescence spectra, (C) photostability under UV light (365 nm), and (D) CIE chromaticity diagram for CD-L.

Cell viability (MTT assay) of human skin fibroblast lineage (HFF1) treated with CD-L (100–1000 µg/mL). Bars represent the mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM) of triplicates performed in the assay. Data are expressed as % viable cells compared to the control group (DMEM). Results were analyzed using GraphPad Prism software using ANOVA followed by Tukey's post-test, **p < 0.01 and ****p < 0.0001 (DMEM vs. treatment).
A) Photoluminescence spectra and B) solid state UV–Vis absorption spectra for Fim-L.
A-B) Image before and after exposure to ultraviolet light, C) flexibility tests and D) transparency for Film-L.

A) Emission spectra of Film-L in the range 298–333 K with excitation at 330 nm and B) Temperature dependence of the intensity (I435 nm, blue line) (linear dependence with R 2 = 0.996) and relative thermal sensitivity S r (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Temperature variation of the intensity (I425nm) as obtained during 4 consecutive heating/cooling cycles.
O2 ●- and HOCl scavenging potential (IC50, mean ± SD) of CD-L.

head.ce:title: Lemon-derived carbon dots as antioxidant and light emitter in fluorescent films applied to nanothermometry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of luminescent nanomaterials for the development of nanothermometers with high sensitivity and free of potentially toxic metals has developed in several fields, such as optoelectronics, sensors, and bioimaging. In addition, luminescent nanothermometers have advantages related to non-invasive measurement, with their wide detection range and high spatial resolution at the nano/microscale. Our study is the first, to our knowledge, to demonstrate a detailed study of a fluorescent film (Film-L) thermal sensor based on carbon dots derived from lemon bagasse extract (CD-L). The CD-L properties were explored as an antioxidant agent; their cytotoxicity was evaluated by using a human non-tumoral skin fibroblast (HFF-1) cell line from an MTT assay. The CD-L were characterized by HRTEM, DLS, FTIR, UV–VIS, and fluorescence spectroscopy. These confirmed their particle size distribution below 10 nm, graphitic structure in the core and surface organic groups, and strong blue emission. The CD-L showed cytocompatibility behavior and scavenging potential reactive species of biological importance: O2 •− and HOCl, with IC50 of 276.8 ± 4.0 and 21.6 ± 0.7, respectively. The Film-L emission intensities (I425 nm) are temperature-dependent in the 298 to 333 K range. The Film-L luminescent thermometer shows a maximum relative thermal sensitivity of 2.69 % K−1 at 333 K.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are luminescent carbon nanoparticles with sizes < 10 nm and quasi-spherical morphology, composed of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons associated with the core and surface defects[1–3]. CDs exhibit excellent optical properties with a broad emission range in the visible spectrum, with high volume of distribution in water, biocompatibility, and photostability [4,5]. They can be produced by simple synthetic routes and are free of toxic metals [1,2,6]. Early CDs studies involved the use of methods and precursors that were expensive [6–8].

To decrease environmental impact and facilitate synthetic routes to produce CDs, the use of green precursors and simple methods has increased since 2014, after Jiang and co-workers used coffee grounds as the carbon source and obtained remarkable results related to cells and fish imaging [9]. It is very important to improve the use of methods and green precursors to obtain CDs that are aligned with the market demands and availability, and meet the needs of the diverse fields of application for these nanoparticles [10]. The potential of CDs as fluorescent sensors is unquestionable, since it ranges from the sensing of ions, molecules, and solvents, to pH and temperature [11–15].

Temperature is a crucial parameter in chemical and biological systems [16]. Ways of measuring temperature have been developed over the years. The search for more accurate thermal sensors that are non-invasive, resistant to heat and respond quickly has been constant [14,17]. The development of these nanoparticle-based materials has been highlighted due to their sensitive detection capability and potential uses in many fields of engineering, agriculture, and medicine [18].

CDs have been widely explored as fluorescent nanothermometers; nevertheless, there is a gap in the literature related to the study of thermometry of CDs in the solid state [14,19]. This is due to the emission from CDs usually due to a “quenching effect” related to the agglomeration of the nanoparticles in the solid state, which promotes the self-fluorescence resulting in non-radiative emissions [17,19]. In 2019, Taspika and co-workers synthesized CDs from citric acid and urea and incorporated them into a PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) matrix for a comparative study of the optical properties between CDs incorporated into PVA (CD s/PVA) and CDs in a solution, showing that the CDs/PVA promoted an increase in quantum yield and lifetime. In addition, the film was also evaluated at different temperatures showing an fluorescence decreased with increasing temperature [20].

Zhang and co-workers (2020) also developed a film of carbon dots derived from N-Aminoethylpiperazine and citric acid, but using epoxy resin as support. This film was applied as a temperature and Hg2+ sensor showing a fluorescence decreased with temperature increased [21]. Both studies have shown promising results of solid-state CDs as a temperature sensor. The stability and reversibility of the film, as well as its relative and absolute thermal sensitivity studies remain to be studied as they are critical to determine the material's efficiency in nanothermometric applications [22]. Currently, CD studies aim to obtain multifunctional properties, therefore, different properties are often explored. The evaluation of antioxidant potential of CD has been evaluated recently, through different studies [23]. Interest has risen mainly due to the potential applications offered by antioxidants, such as prevention of oxidation in food and pharmaceutical products, anti-aging effect in skin care products and application in health against pathological processes mediated by oxidative stress [15,24–26].
In this study, we report the simple and low-cost synthesis of carbon dots from the carbonaceous residues of Tahiti lemon (citrus latifolia), and their use as nanothermometer and antioxidant agents.

Tahiti lemons (Citrus latifolia) were obtained from public markets/supermarkets in the city of Alagoas, Brazil. Glycerol and PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) 99+% hydrolyzed quinine sulfate and sulfuric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultrapure water (18.8 MΩ·cm) was obtained using a Master System MS2000 ultra-purifier (Gehaka, Brazil). SisGen access number: A4098F3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots derived from lemon bagasse (CD-L)
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the carbon nanoparticles, 60 g of Tahiti lemon pomace and 130 mL of deionized water were placed in an Erlenmeyer flask, kept under stirring, and heated at 100 °C for 60 min [27]. The resulting solution was filtered through filter paper and 50 mL of the filtrate (liquid phase) was dried in a domestic microwave oven to produce the CDs for 5 min at 720 W. Following, the CDs purification process was carried out using 50 mL of distilled water was added to solubilize the system and the supernatant was centrifuged at 15,000 RPM for 10 min, and finally filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove the larger particles. The liquid collected material was named CD-L.

The preparation of the luminescent film was based on the dissolution of 150 mg of PVA in 4 mL of distilled water under stirring at 85 °C for 5 min. After cooling, 100 µL of CD-L and 80 µL of glycerol were added to the previously prepared solution. Afterwards, the material was stirred for 2 min and 3 mL of the solution was deposited in a plastic petri dish (35 mm × 10 mm), which was kept in an Odontobrás EL Max oven (model EL 1.3) at 50 °C for 48 h [28,29].

The spectrum in the infrared region (FTIR) was recorded with a Nicolet Thermo Scientific spectrometer. For the acquisition of the pellets, 100 µL of CD-L was dripped onto 20 mg of KBr and kept in the oven at 100 °C for 24 h. The ultraviolet and visible (UV–VIS) absorption spectroscopy was performed on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (UV–VIS-NIR).

The photoluminescence properties were determined in a Horiba-Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer with a continuous 450 W xenon lamp and UV xenon flash tube for excitation, double-grating monochromator in the excitation and UV–Vis (ultraviolet–visible) emission position, single-grating monochromator in the NIR (near infrared) emission position, R928P, and H10330A-75 Hamamatsu photomultipliers, respectively, for the UV–Vis and NIR range emissions. All emission spectra were corrected by the spectral response of the monochromators using a silicon photodiode reference detector to monitor and compensate for variation in the xenon lamp output, using typical correction spectra provided by the manufacturer. The photostability measurements were recorded using the xenon lamp of the Horiba-Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer as the irradiation source, with a fixed wavelength set at 365 nm.

The temperature measurements were carried out using a temperature control module. This was fixed to the horizontal specimen holder coupled to a Horiba-Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer with a 450 W xenon lamp and UV xenon flash tube for continuous excitation as previously mentioned. This temperature controller was configured to operate from room temperature (298 K) up to 343 K with estimated temperature stability better than 1 K.

The relative quantum yield (QY) of the carbon dots was determined based on a method reported in the literature [30]. Quinine sulfate was used as a reference (QY = 54% ± 0.2%) in a 0.1 M sulfuric acid standard solution. The quantum yield of the CD-L (in water) was calculated using the Eq. (1) below:
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ
ST
Grad
x
Grad
ST
η
x
2
η
ST
2
Where the subscript “x” and “st” refers to CDs and standard solution of quinine sulfate. The symbol Φ is the QY and Grad the gradient of the linear regression analysis, and η is the refractive index. Since the solvent used for the CD-L was water, the refractive index is 1.33.

A MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] assay was used to evaluate the cell viability of the human skin fibroblast lineage (HFF1) treated with CD-L. The cells were maintained in a CO2 incubator oven at 310 K in Dulbecos Modified Eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 15% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 µg/mL penicillin, and 100 U/mL streptomycin, plus 1.5 mM l-glutamine. To perform the assay, 3.5 × 103 cells were plated (DMEM/SBF5%) and kept in an incubator for 20 h for their adhesion. HFF-1 cells were then treated with the CD-L at concentrations of 100, 500, or 1000 µg/mL for 24 h. After the exposure time, 23 µL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and the plate was kept in the incubator for 3 h. Then the supernatant was discarded and 150 µL of DMSO/well was added to reveal the formazan crystals formed. Finally, the optical density (OD) was observed at 540 nm wavelength using a spectrophotometer, obtaining the optical density (OD) values (Viability (%) = (OD of treated cells/DO of untreated cells) × 100). For statistical analysis of viable cells results, the ANOVA one-way test was used and data were compared using Tukey’s method. The statistically significant difference accepted was p < 0.05.

The HOCl scavenging activity was performed according to Lucas et al. (2021) [31]. An HOCl solution was prepared at the time of analysis by adjusting the pH of a 1% (v/v) NaOCl solution to 6.2 with H2SO4 addition. The reaction system consisted of the addition of phosphate buffer (100 mM, pH 7.4), CD-L solution (3,12–100 µg mL−1), dihydrorodamine solution (DHR, 5 µM), and HOCl (5 µM). Fluorescence measurements were performed in a microplate reader (Infinite® 200 PRO, TECAN, Männedorf, Switzerland), at 310 K, at wavelengths of 505 ± 10 nm and 530 ± 10 nm, for excitation and emission, respectively.

The radical scavenging activity of superoxide anion was determined according to Lucas et al. (2021) [31]. In a 96-well plate, the following solutions were added to the indicated final concentrations: CD-L (3.12 µg/mL to 800 µg/mL), NADH (166 µM), 4-nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT, 43.3 µM) and N-methylphenazonium methyl sulfate (PMS, 2.7 µM). Potassium phosphate buffer (19 mM, pH 7.4) was used to dissolve CD-L, NADH, NBT, and PMS. Quercetin was used as a standard for comparison purposes. The experiment was conducted at 310 K in a microplate reader (Infinite® 200 PRO, TECAN, Männedorf, Switzerland), and the absorbance was measured at 560 nm.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images show that the maximum number of the CD-L present an average distribution average size of 3.1 ± 0.1 nm and well-dispersed spherical shape (Fig. 1 A–D). Fig. 1C reveals the high crystallinity of nanoparticles with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm showing a positive correlation with the (102) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [1].

FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 ) revealed the presence of main functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. The CD-L shows a broad and intense band at around 3440 cm−1 assigned to OH stretching vibrations. On other hand, CH bond stretches (sp3) are observed around 2930 cm−1. In addition, peaks at 1720, 1400, and 1200 cm−1 reveal the presence of CO, CC, and CN, respectively, associated with oxygenated groups on the surface of the carbon dots, arising from polyaromatics in the graphitic core and nitrogen compounds on the surface [32,33]. The surface functionalization of CD-L occurred from organic compounds from the lemon, such as citric acid, ascorbic acid, and some vitamins (A, B6, B12, E) [27].

Fig. 3 shows the UV–VIS absorption spectrum of the CD-L. The absorption peak reveals a broad band centered at 226 nm related to CC bonds from the graphitic core of the carbon dots [32,33]. The other two absorption peaks centered at 278 and 320 nm correspond to the CO and CN bonds of the oxygen and nitrogen groups on the nanoparticle surface, respectively [32–34].

The emission spectrum for CD-L revealed a broad band centered at 446 nm (under λex = 336 nm) and also presented an independent emission spectrum of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 4 A). This independent profile is observed in the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4B) which was associated with the energy levels of the groups anchored on the surface of CD-L [6,35]. CD-L showed excellent photostability (Fig. 4C) and relative quantum yield (QY) of 10% using quinine sulfate as the reference (54%) [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay and ROS species evaluation of CD-L
ce:section.ce:para: A cell viability interference study was performed using a human non-tumoral skin fibroblast (HFF-1) cell line. Fig. 5 shows the results of the MTT assay for testing the cytocompatibility of the CD-L in human dermal fibroblasts cells (vs. negative control, i.e., DMEM group). After 24 h of incubation with the sample, the results showed that up to a concentration of 1000 µg/mL, the cells showed viability greater than 85 %, indicating biocompatibility of the CD-L. The results obtained corroborate with the literature when they generally attribute the low cytotoxicity of this class of nanoparticles to other non-tumoral cell lines [36,37].

CDs from black soybeans, synthesized through pyrolysis, showed a lower capacity to eliminate O2 •− when compared to CD-L, with an IC50 greater than 400 µg mL−1 [39]. CDs prepared using Scutellaria barbata (SB) and Herba Hedyotis diffusae (HHD) through a hydrothermal method, showed a high potential for O2 •− scavenging, inhibiting 100% of the reactive species at a concentration of only 10 µg mL−1 [40].

The antioxidant properties of CDs can vary depending on the nature of the reactive species and the specific class of CDs. Different mechanisms of action have been attributed to CDs, such as hydrogen transfer from exposed functional groups on their surface, electron transfer, or the formation of radical adducts at sp2 carbon sites. These mechanisms can occur individually or synergistically, increasing the scavenging potential [41]. For CD-L, antioxidant properties can occur especially by hydrogen transfer and electron transfer due to the presence of functional groups on its surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature-Dependent Photoluminescence properties of the Film-L
ce:section.ce:para: There is a gap in the literature regarding the emission of carbon dots emission obtained in the solid state because these nanoparticles are typically self-quenching due to aggregation. Once the particles adjacent to the carbon dots become close, the energy transfer between them becomes excessive, which causes the fluorescence to decrease [19]. The fluorescent film produced in this work decreased the aggregation effects and preserved the optical properties of the carbon dots immobilized in a solid state [20,21]. Fig. 6 A shows a small red shift in emission spectra of Film-L compared to the colloidal aqueous solution of CD-L nanoparticles. In addition, the Film-L exhibits low absorbance between 400 and 700 nm, ensuring its high transparency, as demonstrated by the solid-state UV–Vis spectrum.(Fig. 6B).

Nevertheless, the Film-L possessed similar behavior to the colloidal suspension of CD-L, with transparent coloration in day-light and bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. In addition, the luminescent film has considerable flexibility and transparency, as shown in Fig. 7 .

To study the thermometric behavior of carbon dots, fluorescence spectra were recorded to monitor emission intensity against temperature variation (Fig. 8A). The data showed a gradual decrease in luminescence intensity with increasing temperature, evidencing dependence on temperature. This temperature dependence presented by the CDs can be attributed to the thermal activation that leads to increased non-radiative decay [14,42]. Fig. 8B (blue line) shows a linear relationship between the highest fluorescence intensity (set at 425 nm) by temperature, in the range 298 to 333 K, with an R2 = 0.996. Being linear enabled calculation of the relative thermal sensitivity (Sr) of the nanothermometer regardless of its precursor material (defined as Sr = 1/Δ |∂Δ/∂T|), and giving a value of Sr = 2.69 % K−1 at 298 K (Fig. 8 B; red line). To our knowledge, there are no reports on types of carbon dots that have been incorporated into a film for the temperature sensor.

Fig. 9 shows the temperature variation of the Film-L intensity as obtained (I435 nm) during the heating and cooling cycles. From this data, the thermometer reproducibility was calculated to be 98.6%, demonstrating that the maximum experimental deviation is not higher than 1.4%.

In this work, the carbon dots obtained by a fast and simple method were shown to be highly fluorescent, with blue emission independent of the excitation wavelength, in addition to excellent dispersion in water and photostability. The carbon dots also showed temperature-dependent fluorescence, with intensity reduced gradually with increasing temperature, with a high relative thermal sensitivity of 2.69 % K−1 at 298 K and was stability over four cycles. The CDs were also efficient in capturing reactive oxygen species. The viability of HFF-1 cells was higher than 85%. Finally, CD-L is an excellent candidate for future applications of chemical and biological importance.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

Livia E. da Silva: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Orlando Lucas de L. Calado: Methodology, Investigation. Steffano Felix de Oliveira Silva: Methodology, Investigation. Kleyton Ritomar Monteiro da Silva: Methodology, Investigation. James Henrique Almeida: Methodology, Investigation. Messias de Oliveira Silva: Methodology, Investigation. Rodrigo da Silva Viana: Investigation. Jamylle Nunes de Souza Ferro: Investigation. Jadriane de Almeida Xavier: Investigation. Cintya. D.A.E.S. Barbosa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank at the Universidade Federal de Alagoas (UFAL). The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Brazilian research funding agencies CAPES (Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel) for fellowships (88887.636268/2021-00) and FAPEAL (N◦: 60030.0000002294/2022) for financial support. They also acknowledge the staff of the Earth Rare Laboratory - BSTR (dQF-UFPE) for the execution of fluorescence experiments. RSV express their appreciation for fellowships granted by FAPEAL/CNPq (grant no. DCR2022051000008). The authors would like to thank T. Oliveira dos Santos and Laboratório Multiusuário de Microscopia de Alta Resolução (LabMic) of University of Goiás, Brazil, for HRTEM measurements. The English text of this paper has been revised by Sidney Pratt, Canadian, MAT (The Johns Hopkins University), RSAdip - TESL (Cambridge University).
